name: The Adityas
description: The Adityas are a group of solar deities in Hindu mythology, considered to be the offspring of Aditi, the mother of the gods, and the sage Kashyapa. They are celestial beings associated with light, truth, righteousness, and cosmic order (Rita). Initially, the number of Adityas was eight, but this later expanded to twelve, each representing a month of the year and embodying different aspects of the sun's power. Prominent among them are Surya (the Sun god), Varuna (the god of cosmic order and justice), Mitra (the god of friendship and contracts), Bhaga (the god of wealth and prosperity), Aryaman (the god of guardianship and oaths), Daksha (the god of skill and competence), Ansha (the god of inheritance and portions), Savitr (the god of stimulation and creativity), Tvashtr (the divine craftsman), Pushan (the nourisher and protector), Vishnu (preserver), and Indra (king of gods, sometimes included). The Adityas are guardians of morality and upholders of cosmic law, ensuring balance and harmony in the universe. They are often invoked for blessings of prosperity, protection, and spiritual enlightenment. They are powerful deities who represent different facets of the sun and its life-giving energies, embodying divine justice, cosmic order, and the triumph of light over darkness. Their role is to maintain the balance of the cosmos and ensure the well-being of all beings.
subtitle: Solar deities, sons of Aditi

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Adityas
keyMyths.description: Aditi, weary of bearing imperfect children, prayed intensely and gave birth to the perfect Adityas. They represent the epitome of divine perfection and cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: Varuna's Cosmic Rule
keyMyths.description: Varuna, an important Aditya, is the guardian of Rita, the cosmic order, and ensures that laws are upheld. He uses his noose to bind those who violate this order.

keyMyths.title: Indra's inclusion
keyMyths.description: While Indra is traditionally the king of gods, in some interpretations he is included as one of the twelve Adityas. Representing strength and leadership.
